sourceQuestion:
A binary tree with n > 1 nodes has n_1, n_2, and n_3 nodes of degree one, two and three respectively. The degree of a node is defined as the number of its neighbours. n_1 can be expressed as:
A. n_3 + 1
B. n_3
C. [(n_3 + 1)/2]
D. n_3 - 1

parsed.stem: A binary tree with n > 1 nodes has n_1, n_2, and n_3 nodes of degree one, two and three respectively. The degree of a node is defined as the number of its neighbours. n_1 can be expressed as:

sourceQuestion:
A binary tree with n > 1 nodes has n_1, n_2, and n_3 nodes of degree one, two and three respectively. The degree of a node is defined as the number of its neighbours.

Starting with the above tree, while there remains a node v of degree two in the tree, add an edge between the two neighbours of v and then remove v from the tree. How many edges will remain at the end of the process?
A. 2*n_3 + n_1 - 2
B. n_3
C. n_1 + n_3 - 1
D. n_3 - 1

parsed.stem:
A binary tree with n > 1 nodes has n_1, n_2, and n_3 nodes of degree one, two and three respectively. The degree of a node is defined as the number of its neighbours.

Starting with the above tree, while there remains a node v of degree two in the tree, add an edge between the two neighbours of v and then remove v from the tree. How many edges will remain at the end of the process?

sourceQuestion:
Let G be the graph with vertices numbered 1 to 100. Two vertices i and j are adjacent if |i - j| = 8 or |i - j| = 12. The number of connected components in G is:
A. 8
B. 4
C. 12
D. 25

parsed.stem: Let G be the graph with vertices numbered 1 to 100. Two vertices i and j are adjacent if |i - j| = 8 or |i - j| = 12. The number of connected components in G is:

sourceQuestion:
An Abstract Data Type (ADT) is:
A. same as an abstract class
B. a data type that cannot be instantiated
C. a data type for which only the operations defined on it can be used, but none else
D. all of the above
sourceAnswer: C. a data type for which only the operations defined on it can be used, but none else

parsed.stem: An Abstract Data Type (ADT) is:

options.text: a data type that cannot be instantiated
a data type for which only the operations defined on it can be used, but none else

sourceAnswer: B. G_2 = (V, E_2) where E_2 = {(u, v) | (v, u) in E}

parsed.stem: Let G = (V, E) be a directed graph where V is the set of vertices and E is the set of edges. Then which one of the following graphs has the same strongly connected components as G?

options.text: G_1 = (V, E_1) where E_1 = {(u, v) | (u, v) not in E}
G_2 = (V, E_2) where E_2 = {(u, v) | (v, u) in E}
G_3 = (V, E_3) where E_3 = {(u, v) | there exists w in V such that (u, w) in E and (w, v) in E}
G_4 = (V, E_4) where E_4 = {(u, v) | there exists a path from u to v and from v to u in G}

sourceQuestion:
Match the items in Column 1 with the items in Column 2 in the following table:

Column 1            | Column 2
--------------------|------------
(p) First In First Out  | (i) Stacks
(q) Lookup Operation    | (ii) Queues
(r) Last In First Out   | (iii) Hash Tables

A. (p)-(ii), (q)-(iii), (r)-(i)
B. (p)-(ii), (q)-(i), (r)-(iii)
C. (p)-(i), (q)-(ii), (r)-(iii)
D. (p)-(i), (q)-(iii), (r)-(ii)

parsed.stem:
Match the items in Column 1 with the items in Column 2 in the following table:

Column 1            | Column 2
--------------------|------------
(p) First In First Out  | (i) Stacks
(q) Lookup Operation    | (ii) Queues
(r) Last In First Out   | (iii) Hash Tables

sourceQuestion:
An advantage of chained hash table (external hashing) over the open addressing scheme is:

A. Worst case complexity of search operations is less
B. Space used is less
C. Deletion is easier
D. None of the above

parsed.stem: An advantage of chained hash table (external hashing) over the open addressing scheme is:

options.text: Worst case complexity of search operations is less

sourceQuestion:
Given the following input (4322, 1334, 1471, 9679, 1989, 6171, 6173, 4199) and the hash function x mod 10, which of the following statements are true?

I. 9679, 1989, 4199 hash to the same value
II. 1471, 6171 hash to the same value
III. All elements hash to the same value
IV. Each element hashes to a different value

A. I only
B. II only
C. I and II only
D. III or IV

parsed.stem:
Given the following input (4322, 1334, 1471, 9679, 1989, 6171, 6173, 4199) and the hash function x mod 10, which of the following statements are true?

I. 9679, 1989, 4199 hash to the same value
II. 1471, 6171 hash to the same value
III. All elements hash to the same value
IV. Each element hashes to a different value

sourceQuestion:
Consider a hash table of size seven, with starting index zero, and a hash function (3x+4) mod 7. Assuming the hash table is initially empty, which of the following is the contents of the table when the sequence 1, 8, 5, 11, 15 is inserted into the table using closed hashing? Note that '-' denotes an empty location in the table.

A. 8, -, 5, 1, 15, -, 11
B. 1, 8, 5, -, -, 11, 15
C. 1, 8, 11, 15, -, 5, -
D. 1, -, 5, 8, 15, 11, -

parsed.stem: Consider a hash table of size seven, with starting index zero, and a hash function (3x+4) mod 7. Assuming the hash table is initially empty, which of the following is the contents of the table when the sequence 1, 8, 5, 11, 15 is inserted into the table using closed hashing? Note that '-' denotes an empty location in the table.

sourceQuestion:
The keys 12, 18, 13, 2, 3, 23, 5 and 15 are inserted into an initially empty hash table of length 10 using open addressing with hash function h(k) = k mod 10 and linear probing. What is the resultant hash table?

A. 0: -, 1: -, 2: 12, 3: 13, 4: 2, 5: 3, 6: 23, 7: 5, 8: 18, 9: 15
B. 0: -, 1: -, 2: 12, 3: 13, 4: 2, 5: 3, 6: 23, 7: 5, 8: 18, 9: -
C. 0: 15, 1: -, 2: 12, 3: 13, 4: 2, 5: 5, 6: -, 7: -, 8: 18, 9: -
D. 0: -, 1: -, 2: 12, 3: 23, 4: 13, 5: 2, 6: 3, 7: 5, 8: 18, 9: 15

parsed.stem: The keys 12, 18, 13, 2, 3, 23, 5 and 15 are inserted into an initially empty hash table of length 10 using open addressing with hash function h(k) = k mod 10 and linear probing. What is the resultant hash table?

sourceQuestion:
A hash table of length 10 uses open addressing with hash function h(k) = k mod 10, and linear probing. After inserting 6 values into an empty hash table, the table is shown as below:

Index | Value
  0   |
  1   |
  2   | 42
  3   | 23
  4   | 34
  5   | 52
  6   | 46
  7   | 33
  8   |
  9   |

Which one of the following choices gives a possible order in which the key values could have been inserted in the table?
A. 46, 42, 34, 52, 23, 33
B. 34, 42, 23, 52, 33, 46
C. 46, 34, 42, 23, 52, 33
D. 42, 46, 33, 23, 34, 52

parsed.stem:
A hash table of length 10 uses open addressing with hash function h(k) = k mod 10, and linear probing. After inserting 6 values into an empty hash table, the table is shown as below:

Index | Value

  1   |
  2   | 42
  3   | 23
  4   | 34
  5   | 52
  6   | 46
  7   | 33
  8   |
  9   |

Which one of the following choices gives a possible order in which the key values could have been inserted in the table?

sourceQuestion:
Consider a hash table with 9 slots. The hash function is h(k) = k mod 9. The collisions are resolved by chaining. The following 9 keys are inserted in the order: 5, 28, 19, 15, 20, 33, 12, 17, 10. The maximum, minimum, and average chain lengths in the hash table, respectively, are
A. 3, 0, and 1
B. 3, 3, and 3
C. 4, 0, and 1
D. 3, 0, and 2

sourceQuestion:
Consider a hash table with 100 slots. Collisions are resolved using chaining. Assuming simple uniform hashing, what is the probability that the first 3 slots are unfilled after the first 3 insertions?
A. (97/100)^3
B. (99 * 98 * 97) / 100^3
C. (97 * 96 * 95) / (12 * 100^3)
D. (99 * 98 * 97) / (12 * 100^3)

parsed.stem: Consider a hash table with 100 slots. Collisions are resolved using chaining. Assuming simple uniform hashing, what is the probability that the first 3 slots are unfilled after the first 3 insertions?

sourceQuestion:
Which one of the following hash functions on integers will distribute keys most uniformly over 10 buckets numbered 0 to 9 for x ranging from 0 to 2020?
A. h(x) = x^2 mod 10
B. h(x) = x^3 mod 10
C. h(x) = (11x^2) mod 10
D. h(x) = (12x^2) mod 10

parsed.stem: Which one of the following hash functions on integers will distribute keys most uniformly over 10 buckets numbered 0 to 9 for x ranging from 0 to 2020?

sourceQuestion:
Consider performing uniform hashing on an open address hash table with load factor alpha = n/m < 1, where n elements are stored in the table with m slots. The expected number of probes in an unsuccessful search is at most 1/(1-alpha). Inserting an element in this hash table requires at most _______ probes, on average.
A. ln(1/(1-alpha)) + 1/(1-alpha)
B. 1/(1-alpha)
C. 1 + 1/(1-alpha)
D. ln(1/(1-alpha))

parsed.stem: Consider performing uniform hashing on an open address hash table with load factor alpha = n/m < 1, where n elements are stored in the table with m slots. The expected number of probes in an unsuccessful search is at most 1/(1-alpha). Inserting an element in this hash table requires at most _______ probes, on average.

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following statement(s) is TRUE?
I. A hash function takes a message of arbitrary length and generates a fixed length code.
II. A hash function takes a message of fixed length and generates a code of variable length.
III. A hash function may give the same hash value for distinct messages.
IV. A hash function takes a message of fixed length and generates a fixed length code.
A. I only
B. I and III only
C. II and IV only
D. II only

parsed.stem:
Which of the following statement(s) is TRUE?
I. A hash function takes a message of arbitrary length and generates a fixed length code.
II. A hash function takes a message of fixed length and generates a code of variable length.
III. A hash function may give the same hash value for distinct messages.
IV. A hash function takes a message of fixed length and generates a fixed length code.

sourceQuestion:
Consider a hash function that distributes keys uniformly. The hash table size is 10. After hashing of how many keys will the probability that any new key hashed collides with an existing one exceed 0.5?

A. 5
B. 6
C. 7
D. 10

parsed.stem: Consider a hash function that distributes keys uniformly. The hash table size is 10. After hashing of how many keys will the probability that any new key hashed collides with an existing one exceed 0.5?

sourceQuestion:
Consider a hash table of size 11 that uses open addressing with linear probing. Let h(k) = k mod 11 be the hash function used. A sequence of records with keys
43 36 92 87 11 4 71 13 14
is inserted into an initially empty hash table, the bins of which are indexed from zero to ten. What is the index of the bin into which the last record is inserted?

A. 3
B. 4
C. 6
D. 7

43 36 92 87 11 4 71 13 14

sourceQuestion:
Compute the postfix equivalent of the following infix arithmetic expression:

a + b * c - d ^ e ^ f / a

Assume ^ is exponentiation. Assume normal operator precedences.

A. abc*+def^^a/-
B. abc*+de^f^a/-
C. abc*+def^^/a-
D. ab+c*de-f^/a^

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following is essential for converting an infix expression to the postfix form efficiently?

A. An operator stack
B. An operand stack
C. An operand stack and an operator stack
D. A parse tree

parsed.stem: Which of the following is essential for converting an infix expression to the postfix form efficiently?

options.text: An operand stack and an operator stack

sourceQuestion:
Assume that the operators +, -, x are left associative and ^ is right associative. The order of precedence (from highest to lowest) is ^, x, +, -. The postfix expression corresponding to the infix expression a + b x c - d ^ e ^ f is:

A. abc x + def ^ ^ -
B. abc x + de ^ f ^ -
C. ab + cx de ^ f ^ -
D. - + a x bc ^ d ^ ef

A. One pointer.
B. Two pointers.
C. Multiple pointers.
D. No pointer.

sourceQuestion:
A list of elements is commonly written as a sequence of elements enclosed in a pair of square brackets. For example, [10, 20, 30] is a list of three elements and [] is a nil list. Five functions are defined below:
- car(l) returns the first element of its argument list l
- cdr(l) returns the list obtained by removing the first element from l
- cons(x, l) returns the list obtained by inserting x at the front of list l
- null(l) returns true if l is a nil list, else false
- atom(x) returns true if x is an atom (not a list), else false

What does the following function compute?
f(l) = if null(l) then [] else cons(car(l), f(cdr(l)))
g(l1, l2) = if null(l1) then l2 else g(cdr(l1), cons(car(l1), l2))

What does g(f([32, 16, 8]), [9, 11, 12]) compute?
A. [8, 9, 11, 12]
B. [8, 16, 32, 9, 11, 12]
C. [32, 16, 8, 9, 11, 12]
D. [9, 11, 12, 8, 16, 32]

parsed.stem:
A list of elements is commonly written as a sequence of elements enclosed in a pair of square brackets. For example, [10, 20, 30] is a list of three elements and [] is a nil list. Five functions are defined below:
- car(l) returns the first element of its argument list l
- cdr(l) returns the list obtained by removing the first element from l
- cons(x, l) returns the list obtained by inserting x at the front of list l
- null(l) returns true if l is a nil list, else false
- atom(x) returns true if x is an atom (not a list), else false

What does the following function compute?
f(l) = if null(l) then [] else cons(car(l), f(cdr(l)))
g(l1, l2) = if null(l1) then l2 else g(cdr(l1), cons(car(l1), l2))

What does g(f([32, 16, 8]), [9, 11, 12]) compute?

sourceQuestion:
Consider a singly linked list having n nodes. The data items d_1, d_2, ..., d_n are stored in these nodes. Let X be a pointer to the j-th node (1 <= j <= n) in which d_j is stored. A new data item d stored in node with address Y is to be inserted. Give an algorithm to insert d into the list to obtain a list having items d_1, d_2, ..., d_j, d, d_{j+1}, ..., d_n in order.
Which of the following is the correct algorithm?
A. Y->next = X->next; X->next = Y
B. X->next = Y->next; Y->next = X
C. Y->next = X; X->next = Y
D. X->next = Y; Y->next = X->next

parsed.stem:
Consider a singly linked list having n nodes. The data items d_1, d_2, ..., d_n are stored in these nodes. Let X be a pointer to the j-th node (1 <= j <= n) in which d_j is stored. A new data item d stored in node with address Y is to be inserted. Give an algorithm to insert d into the list to obtain a list having items d_1, d_2, ..., d_j, d, d_{j+1}, ..., d_n in order.
Which of the following is the correct algorithm?

A. Insertion sort
B. Binary search
C. Radix sort
D. Polynomial manipulation

parsed.stem: Linked lists are not suitable data structures for which one of the following problems?

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following statements is true?
I. As the number of entries in a hash table increases, the number of collisions increases.
II. Recursive programs are efficient.
III. The worst case complexity for Quicksort is O(n^2).
IV. Binary search using a linear linked list is efficient.

A. I and II
B. II and III
C. I and IV
D. I and III

parsed.stem:
Which of the following statements is true?
I. As the number of entries in a hash table increases, the number of collisions increases.
II. Recursive programs are efficient.
III. The worst case complexity for Quicksort is O(n^2).
IV. Binary search using a linear linked list is efficient.

sourceQuestion:
The concatenation of two lists is to be performed on O(1) time. Which of the following implementations of a list should be used?
A. Singly linked list
B. Doubly linked list
C. Circular doubly linked list
D. Array implementation of list

parsed.stem: The concatenation of two lists is to be performed on O(1) time. Which of the following implementations of a list should be used?

sourceAnswer: A. n-1 times; 0 and n-1 times; counts distinct elements

options.text: n-1 times; 0 and n-1 times; counts distinct elements
n-1 times; 0 and n-1 times; counts duplicate elements
n times; 1 and n times; counts distinct elements
n-1 times; 1 and n-1 times; counts distinct elements
